sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

The main item up for discussion at last week’s committee meeting, was how to maximise the return on our savings.  The interest rates available to clubs and associations have always been fairly pathetic, and we currently get a miserable 1.6% from HSBC.

When our neighbouring club, North Yorkshire Sailwings, recommended an account with Redwood that pays 4.1% (variable), we had to consider it carefully.  We were warned that there were quite a few loopholes to jump through in setting up the account, but with the potential to generate an extra £430pa in club funds, it had to be taken seriously.

We did a bit of research.  The Redwood account requires a minimum investment of £10K, and has a notice period of 35 days; neither of which represent a problem.  Redwood is a relatively new online bank launched in 2017 and all accounts are protected by the FSCS.  Trustpilot gave them a rating of 2.5/5 (poor).  Perhaps more significantly their historical interest rates have not been great (2022 – 0.8%; 2023 – 1.7%).

In addition to maximising our return, the committee also has a responsibility for ensuring that our savings are secure.  Threats come in 2 forms: a rogue committee member stealing the money, and the ever-present threat from phishing.  (The Treasurer receives several emails each month purporting to be from the ‘Chairman’, asking him to settle an invoice.)  It’s not that we don’t trust our fellow committee members, but we have to think about the future.  It’s hard enough to find committee members, and we don’t want to attract the wrong sort.  (By way of example the Devon Rowing Club lost £42K when their Treasurer went rogue, and in 2022 the Light Aircraft Association was scammed out of £65K.)

These days the majority of accounts are online.  Armed with a password anyone can clear out the account.  It was for this very reason that we opted for the HSBC savings account in the first place.  It’s not online and requires 2 signatures (Chairman & Secretary) to make a withdrawal.  The Treasurer is not a signatory, thereby spreading club liability even further.

We spent some time discussing how we might ‘enhance’ the security protocols to suit our needs, either by holding half a password each, or sending 2-factor identification codes to a second person; until we realised that they can easily be reset.

In the end we decided to leave things as they are.  With falling interest rates, hopefully we won’t be missing out on £430pa for much longer.

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

It’s Thursday July 18th, the day after I went XC from Hawkswick with Chris Fountain. I’ve just been out for my lunchtime run and the difference between today and yesterday stuck me. I don't mean the wind and the high frontal / wavey stuff coming in. The boundary layer, low down air we fly in. It's mostly down to the heavy band of rain that passed west to east on Tuesday.

On Wednesday there was plenty of instability and there was no delay in the cumulus starting in the morning but the ground was still wet - particularly where our hot air sources are in valley bottoms. The sun was okay heating the damp ground, but it meant that cloud base was low (900m at Hawkswick at about midday), cloud was long lived and extensive - at least 5/8ths coverage in large areas of the Dales. Cloud was deep too, thousands of feet in height. Lastly the humid air meant that cloud didn't evaporate quick enough which lead to extensive spreadout (8/8ths) which cut the sun off from the ground.

Today the humid air has blown through (53% vs yesterday's 80% ish) and the ground has had a day to dry out which has given us a 1500m base, dotted cumulus with short lives, 2/8th coverage and with the wind and extra distance between climbs, great hang gliding conditions :-) The lesson is that two similar RASP predictions (as Wed & Thurs appeared on Tuesday's forecast) for XC stars can mean two fairly different days.

And about that heavy cloud on the Wednesday cross country.  Normally I’m fine around cloud, on alert, but not panicking. A few things had put me on high alert though for Wednesday in the first couple of climbs and I thought I’d share them.

Martin Baxter reports

I was interested to read Pete’s article about Beach Launching in the last edition.  It reminded me of the time I was introduced to the technique, also at Silecroft.  My wife was on hand to take some photos and fortunately I didn’t cock it up, so I wrote a piece for the February 2017 Skywords (p16 - 19) and the (now defunct) BHPA Pilot Development Structure (PDS).  If nothing else you can admire the Chairman’s snazzy flying suit!

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

If you’re not familiar with a site then please study the site guide before visiting, or get a verbal briefing on arrival.  A plea for somebody to drop a pin smacks of poor organisation.  If you are familiar with a site then please check the traffic light system on the website before EVERY visit.  Green - go; Amber - restrictions; Red - Closed.

Sites (North).  Following complaints from the gamekeeper at Nappa Scar, Tom has negotiated some alternative arrangements during the bird breeding season.  Watch out for signs notifying restrictions to the Open Access Land.  We’re aware that the sign at Stags Fell is broken: it’s on Tom’s list.

Thanks to Kevin Woodward for alerting us to the fact that the National Park Authority is using a helicopter to move stone from the Tailbridge car park to Nine Stones Rigg to repair the Coast to Coast path.  The helicopter will be operating on weekdays during the month of August.  There was a NOTAM yesterday, but nothing today when I know they intended to work.  Probably best to avoid Tailbridge on working days this month to avoid wasted travel.  

Sites (South).  Simon has been rather distracted by a sailing trip circumnavigating the UK.  Diesel engine problems mean that his return has been delayed until September(?).  I was at Windbank yesterday.  Watch out for the broken rung on the ladder over the first wall.  Don’t pay £3 for car parking unless challenged.

sections.title: Dales FlyIn and X Dales 2024
sections.content:
Stef Sykes reports

Whilst writing this the sun has come out and the chat is getting lively. A few days of hope!

Not to worry, we all know that September will be the month of glory.

Registration payments for the XDales are hitting the ceiling, however, return of Registration documents is at half mast. If you’ve paid on Eventbrite, but not returned your forms to Ed, please make time to do so.

The XDales Hike and Fly race will take place on one of the days of our now celebrated Dales FlyIn based just outside Settle on the first weekend in September. There are places still available for the Dales FlyIn at https://www.eventbrite.co.uk/e/dales-flyin-2024-tickets. There, you will also be able to buy the last few tickets for the XDales, should you wish to enter.

To note, you will need to buy a ticket to attend the Dales FlyIn if you wish to base yourself at the start and finishing point of the XDales.

The Dales FlyIn features three days of friendly flying with a broad set of coaches providing site briefings from the many hills around the site enabling flights back to the camping field (and into the pool for a bullseye). The weather goddesses are now cognisant of our need for warm dry weather that weekend, so rest assured all will be fine.

sections.title: You’re probably wondering how I ended up in a tree…
sections.content:
Rhian Ebrey reports

Yep, that’s my glider gift wrapping the tree!  I’m not the sort of person to normally take huge risks with my flying, so I didn’t expect to end up with a glider in pieces within my first 25 hours of flying.  Yet, with hindsight, I made mistake after mistake, and so the stars aligned for the making of a great anecdote.

Wednesday 24th July.  I was heading over to Westie’s that evening for some work, and he suggested going out flying that afternoon first.  We drove to Brigsteer, a site I hadn’t flown before.  The conditions seemed good; perfect wind speed and direction, nothing caused me alarm.  We started setting up.  I had just received a new radio, but the manual was useless and I didn’t understand how to set it up properly.  I asked Westie, but he didn’t know either, so we shrugged our shoulders and joked that we’d just shout to each other.  Mistake number one.  Afterwards, Westie had told me that if I’d had a radio, he would have told me not to take off after he launched.

I hadn’t flown for a month, and so while I was waiting for Westie to finish setting up, I decided to ground handle to settle my nerves.  This gave me the first signs that the air wasn’t great, but I shrugged it off as my skills and still being a bit rusty, as I had been in Spring.  I watched Westie launch, and then took off.  I was immediately plucked and then dumped on take off two or three times.  Even though the wind had felt ok on the hill, I was being blown around, and worried that I wouldn’t make it over the low wire fence off to my right that I was getting pushed towards.  It crossed my mind that I should top land and abort the flight while I was still close to the ground.  But I really wanted to fly.  I was frustrated at seeing so many other people out and yet I’ve not had many opportunities to fly this year.  So I pushed on.  Mistake number two.

Once you’ve cleared the launch field, Brigsteer is just a ridge of trees, with big fields out in front to land in.  This lulled me into a false sense of security, thinking that if I was ever in trouble, I could just turn away from the trees and land.  I watched Westie push out from the ridge on his Zeno, although I didn’t register how slowly he was making progress and I didn’t register any alarm that I was struggling to penetrate forward on my low B.  I was still getting plucked up and dropped, although not quite as violently as on take off, so I just assumed I was in lumpy air and it would get better.  Even though I had an escape plan, I couldn’t execute it on my glider.  Mistake number three.

I crabbed along the ridge and tree line, still thinking that my escape plan was good despite still not being able to penetrate forward.  I saw Westie, who was a bit higher than me but not significantly, turn back deeper into the trees towards the cliffs where I’d been told it worked.  I was low, but thought that I’d pick up the lift from the cliffs and then be in the clear.  I followed Westie, taking myself further away from my escape route and into the tree cover.  Mistake number four.

I got to the cliffs, but there was no lift.  Finally, the alarm bells started to go off in my head.  I turned back, away from the cliffs and towards the landing fields which were now quite a way away.  I was low, very low.  As had been consistently happening, I was dropping height rapidly with sudden dumps, a few wing tip collapses, but none of the lift from before.  Now, it was a case of which tree did I want to hit.  Thinking again of my working but useless radio in the back of my harness, I shouted across to Westie, who was further along the ridge, hoping he’d hear me and spot that I was about to make a tree landing.  I’d spotted a gravel track through the woods, and lined up to try and land along it.  I was being blown around by now, struggling but managed to just keep on my course.  I was suddenly coming in fast along the track.  I had a passing thought of pulling big ears to try and protect my glider through the narrow passage of the track, but my speed now meant that I was likely to hit the ground hard (as well as it not being advisable to pull big ears on landing anyway as you then can’t flare).  Luckily, I almost made it to the ground when my glider finally wrapped itself around a tree, my toes were even touching the ground as I got out of my harness.  Does that still count as a tree landing then??  Luckily, I was fine if a bit bruised from the swing back into the tree that had grabbed my glider, but my glider sustained quite a bit of damage.  But I walked away safe, and having learnt a lot of lessons.

After a debrief with Westie, he also thinks that the wind direction changed during the flight; he’d seen a third pilot take off, and take a significant collapse just off launch.  This might have accounted for some of the wing tip collapses I had, but still didn’t excuse the numerous warning signs that I’d missed.  So, what were my top (of many!) lessons learnt:

  1. Your radio is for more than just chatting on or, in the case with my old radio, screaming white noise at me until I turned it off mid-flight anyway.
  2. Being frustrated at not being able to fly doesn’t mean you carry on if the conditions aren’t good.
  3. I hadn’t given full respect to the conditions on take off.  I’d had a flight last year in the Rockies which had pushed my bump tolerance, and so I didn’t consider these conditions to be as scary as that, and was therefore ok.  Even though I wasn’t high over a big rocky landscape, this site’s hazards (lots of trees!), made this still a site to respect.
  4. Stay within reach of (and ability to use) your escape route.

John Westall reports

The tree incident 24/07/2024

As a reasonably experienced pilot, one sometimes forgets about how it was back in the green room. This I expect is pretty normal and I imagine our coaches have to aim off for this!

I’m a believer in the ethos of the thinking pilot strategy, which means you as a qualified CP or Pilot, make calls for yourself on whether to fly or not.

However I feel partially responsible this time around, why ?

So I invite Rhian to come flying as a little treat before we do some work. I’m getting intel on various sites to go to during the day and this is changing, due to the fast pace of the changing weather.

I decided to try Brigsteer as it’s top drivable and a reasonably low lying launch, this indicates that the wind has increased!

We arrive and it feels light and bang on the hill. I brief Rhian on the site setup, landing fields, areas of lift and danger areas.

We set out and already the conditions have changed. The wind feels more punchy!however this can be normal right, is it just a gust or a thermal coming through? it’ll probably settle soon.

I launch, and having told Rhian I’ll lead the way thinking this will give her some confidence and a route to follow. My penetration is already slow, and I wonder if Rhian has seen this ? She hasn’t and launches.

I watch as she pushes over the trees, all is good and I relax. I push over the cliffs and soar high, Rhian follows but for some reason doesn’t connect to any lift she ends up low over the trees! She makes a pretty good landing on a very tricky track through a rotary tree line. The right hand edge just catches a tree and she swings round and lands safely.

I call in the cavalry and a ladder !! While I was top landing, which is pretty much where we took off from, the wind wasn’t on the hill any longer; it was cross, and much stronger and rougher.

A third pilot was launching as I landed, brave soul, he was paid in kind for this with three collapses! the last being at least fifty percent. He very quickly realised that the conditions were very tricky and bottom landed.

In conclusion although Rhian has been very honest of her mistakes the changing conditions also made life tough.

She also was flying with a seasoned mountain pilot which I guess gives a wrong impression of the conditions, as I’m on hotter wing etc.

Ultimately the warning signs were there even before we launched, they were changing slowly all day. Moving from a state of calm and good flying conditions to a state of challenging and strong conditions! Guessing sometimes it’s best to sit this one out as we don’t know how bad the conditions will deteriorate, we’ve all heard that before haven’t we 🫣

sections.title: AIRPROX?
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

You may recall, from the last edition of Skywords that, following an exchange with my contact at RAF Leeming about MATZ incursions, I casually asked him if the jets that we saw over Hawes on 26 Jun had seen us.  It turns out that they were USAF F35s.  (Tracklogs courtesy of Ed Cleasby.)

I’ve just had the following response from the USAF Liaison Officer:

‘The crews reported diverting off their low level route (further North) due to weather. The crews had intended to over fly the valley when they sighted the paragliders in the air. On sighting they descend below the paragliders into the valley to increase separation. As they had diverted off their planned route they had not seen the NOTAM prior to the flight. Had they seen it they would have given it a wide berth.  The crews have briefed this risk within the squadron and developed their processes when diverting.’

My main aim in all this was to raise awareness amongst military pilots that they don’t own the class G airspace below 2,000’ AGL.  I’ve briefed those at RAF Leeming about this several times, but a reminder does no harm.

In turn we should all be aware that Wensleydale is a low-level transit route.  As the proud owner of a cottage in Hawes I see/hear them nearly every weekday.  Flying at between 500’ - 2,000’ down the middle of the valley puts you into the risk band.  It strikes me that Semer Water is at more of a risk than most sites since going over the back puts you into Wensleydale.  (I’ve also had 3 Typhoons fly underneath me near Hawes, having departed from Brant Side.)

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content: Martin Baxter suggests that little has changed since 1989.  In those days the military HAD to AVOID our notifications, but still didn’t.

sections.title: First Aid Course update
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

A really quick update before this edition goes out as I've just heard back from the preferred instructor who has just come back from expeditions to Laos and Morocco (from my phone as my laptop suffered a fatal Blue Screen of Death following Windows 24H2 update, so forgive my brevity and typos).

The proposed date is Saturday 2 November, at the Bradley Village Hall (near Skipton) and led by Allan Shaw.

Costs look like they are going to be around £65 pp for 10 people, less with 12!  Possible club support is still under discussion, but at this price it's already very good value!

If you're interested, please get your name down by contacting me at firstaid@dhpc.org.uk or WA or Telegram.

Bradley village hall: https://bradleyvillagehall.org.uk/

Allan Shaw: https://ashawexperiencefirstaid.co.uk/

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

Welcome to the launch of the new “PG Tips” section…

This section is for anyone who feels they can contribute with a tip or two whatever level you are at, either email

Me sjcraven.z@gmail.com and I will enter your tips or contact Joseph :newsletter@dhpc.org.uk and

he will give you access to Skywords.

subsections.title: This month's Tips: Keeping your wing and harness attached.
subsections.content:
Wind coming from this edge of photo.

How to get your wing out of the harness to launch.

  1. When I launch I twist to the left so when I get the wing out I arrange the lines so the left risers go over

The right risers if you twist to the right then the right risers go over the top.

  1.                     Wind coming from the left of photo

            Move your harness away to put tension on the lines, if it’s windy don’t unfurl the whole wing.

                                 

 I am taking these photos on my own but I hope you can see that the A lines are going underneath the others

To sort this out ………..

4)

You need to give your harness a back flip, if the lines went over the top and round the other lines

You would give your harness a forward flip.

5)

Now you have the A lines on top as they should be and all the other lines will be correct

With no twists though you will still need to look out for tension knots which can be done as

You create a wall with your wing.

Thanks to Adam Cox for providing the photo of Paragliding Monkey.

Stephen Craven.
